sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Otitis externa is inflammation or infection of the external auditory canal, often associated with moisture, local trauma, or debris. Prehospital care should focus on confirming the likely canal source, excluding red flags, providing analgesia, and arranging appropriate follow-up or escalation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Ear pain, often worse when the pinna or tragus is moved
- Itch, fullness, reduced hearing, or otorrhoea
- Erythema or oedema of the external canal
- Fungal infection may present with itch, debris, and less severe pain than bacterial infection

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Red flags include cranial nerve findings, spreading cellulitis, severe systemic illness, immunosuppression, diabetes, severe canal swelling preventing topical therapy, or concern for malignant otitis externa
- If the tympanic membrane is perforated or status is uncertain, avoid potentially ototoxic topical agents unless specifically directed
- Do not syringe the ear
- Severe pain out of proportion, granulation tissue, or persistent night pain should lower the threshold for urgent escalation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey only if clinically indicated, then focused ear assessment
- Inspect the external ear and perform gentle otoscopy if tolerated
- Provide simple analgesia
- Keep the ear dry and avoid further trauma such as cotton buds or scratching
- Consider simple acetic-acid or drying preparations only where appropriate and the tympanic membrane is thought to be intact
- Escalate early if examination is limited by swelling or if diabetes, immunocompromise, or spreading infection is present

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Topical antimicrobial or antifungal drops may be considered within local protocol or after doctor consultation
- An ear wick may be appropriate for marked canal oedema if within scope and supported locally
- Escalate for oral or IV therapy only when there is spreading infection, severe pain, systemic illness, or failure of topical management
- Avoid repeated local manipulation when malignant otitis externa or deeper infection is a concern

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Reassessment is important if symptoms are worsening or not improving over 48 to 72 hours
- Pain out of proportion, granulation tissue, or cranial neuropathy should prompt urgent specialist consideration
